name: Camp Young (DTC HQ)
description: The Camp Young (DTC HQ) served as a primary training installation under the Desert Training Center, where the IV Armored Corps conducted rigorous chemical warfare defense drills and signal corps communications training under blackout conditions between 1942 and 1943. General Patton established the DTC in April 1942 specifically to harden troops for North African desert warfare; daytime temperatures regularly exceeded 111°F and water was strictly rationed to simulate combat conditions. Troops lived in pyramidal tents on gravel pads and were forbidden fires at night to maintain light discipline across the open desert. The site today falls within the BLM Palm Springs-South Coast Field Office and surface-collecting is permitted with a free BLM permit. Metal detectorists working the camp perimeters and adjacent vehicle parks report finding .30-06 Springfield casings, signal corps wire spools, and shovel handle hardware.
source: Desert Training Center Historical Survey / NARA RG 337

General Patton established the DTC in April 1942 specifically to harden troops for North African desert warfare; daytime temperatures regularly exceeded 111°F and water was strictly rationed to simulate combat conditions. Troops lived in pyramidal tents on gravel pads and were forbidden fires at night to maintain light discipline across the open desert. The site today falls within the BLM Tucson Field Office (AZ) and surface-collecting is permitted with a free BLM permit. Metal detectorists working the camp perimeters and adjacent vehicle parks report finding Enfield .303 brass, mess-kit fragments, uniform buttons, and dog-tag chains.
source: Desert Training Center Historical Survey / NARA RG 337

name: DTC Wellton Firing Range
description: The DTC Wellton Firing Range served as a primary training installation under the Desert Training Center, where the 3rd Infantry Division conducted rigorous half-track convoy exercises across open desert flats and motorized infantry assault tactics between 1943 and 1944. General Patton established the DTC in April 1942 specifically to harden troops for North African desert warfare; daytime temperatures regularly exceeded 113°F and water was strictly rationed to simulate combat conditions. Troops lived in pyramidal tents on gravel pads and were forbidden fires at night to maintain light discipline across the open desert. The site today falls within the Bureau of Land Management Needles Field Office and surface-collecting is permitted with a free BLM permit. Metal detectorists working the camp perimeters and adjacent vehicle parks report finding M1 Garand brass, .30-caliber belt links, tank track pins, and ration tin fragments.
source: Desert Training Center Historical Survey / NARA RG 337

name: DTC Dome Valley Training Area
description: The DTC Dome Valley Training Area served as a primary training installation under the Desert Training Center, where the 2nd Armored Division conducted rigorous night-movement exercises under radio silence and tank-destroyer ambush tactics between 1942 and 1944. General Patton established the DTC in April 1942 specifically to harden troops for North African desert warfare; daytime temperatures regularly exceeded 120°F and water was strictly rationed to simulate combat conditions. Troops lived in pyramidal tents on gravel pads and were forbidden fires at night to maintain light discipline across the open desert. The site today falls within the BLM Palm Springs-South Coast Field Office and surface-collecting is permitted with a free BLM permit. Metal detectorists working the camp perimeters and adjacent vehicle parks report finding .30-06 Springfield casings, signal corps wire spools, and shovel handle hardware.
source: Desert Training Center Historical Survey / NARA RG 337

source: Desert Training Center Historical Survey / NARA RG 337

name: DTC Kofa Range Maneuver Sector 1
description: Grid sector 1 of the DTC Kofa Range Maneuver Sector was used by the 85th Infantry Division for engineer obstacle-course construction exercises from 1943 to 1944 under the Desert Training Center command. Soldiers endured extreme heat, sandstorms, and limited water to simulate the North African campaign environment that Patton was preparing his forces for. Ammunition expended in this sector included small-arms, artillery, and vehicle ordnance. The site lies on open BLM Tucson Field Office (AZ) land accessible by existing desert two-tracks. Detecting surveys in comparable DTC sectors have recovered .30 M1 carbine brass, gasoline-drum bung wrenches, and khaki uniform buttons.
source: BLM Cultural Resources Survey / NARA RG 337

name: Fort Ord North Range Maneuver Area
description: The Fort Ord North Range Maneuver Area was used by the 8th Infantry Division for amphibious landing drills on Monterey Bay beaches between 1942-1945. The site represents a key piece of the continental US training infrastructure that prepared American forces for the multiple theaters of World War II. Soldiers trained under conditions designed to replicate the specific environment where their division was expected to deploy, with instructors returning from combat to update training doctrine in real time. The site is now on public or state land and the training footprint is visible in historical aerial photography. Detectorists working old camp roads and assembly areas have documented 1944-dated wheat-cent coinage, M1 helmet liner fragments, and .30-caliber M1 Garand brass.
source: NARA Record Group 337 / Army Historical Division

The site represents a key piece of the continental US training infrastructure that prepared American forces for the multiple theaters of World War II. Soldiers trained under conditions designed to replicate the specific environment where their division was expected to deploy, with instructors returning from combat to update training doctrine in real time. The site is now on public or state land and the training footprint is visible in historical aerial photography. Detectorists working old camp roads and assembly areas have documented .30-06 M1 Garand brass, tank-destroyer data plates, and 1942-dated coinage.

name: DTC Grid Maneuver Site 1 (AZ)
description: DTC grid-reference maneuver site 1 (AZ) hosted elements of the 9th Armored Division for chemical warfare defense drills exercises during 1943–1944. These dispersed camp areas were used as temporary bivouacs between large-scale maneuvers, allowing troops to cycle through field-living conditions across hundreds of square miles. Soldiers moved by vehicle convoy between numbered grid squares on improvised desert roads that are still visible in USGS historical topographic maps. The area is presently open BLM land managed by the BLM Lake Havasu Field Office. Past detecting sessions in comparable DTC grid areas have produced .50-cal links, 105mm artillery brass primers, and Willys jeep frame hardware.
source: BLM Cultural Resources Survey / NARA RG 337

These dispersed camp areas were used as temporary bivouacs between large-scale maneuvers, allowing troops to cycle through field-living conditions across hundreds of square miles. Soldiers moved by vehicle convoy between numbered grid squares on improvised desert roads that are still visible in USGS historical topographic maps. The area is presently open BLM land managed by the BLM Lake Havasu Field Office. Past detecting sessions in comparable DTC grid areas have produced Signal Corps field telephone parts, radio chassis fragments, and vehicle data tags.
source: BLM Cultural Resources Survey / NARA RG 337
